items.q: Why did Britain pursue appeasement in the 1930s?
items.correct: Britain was militarily unprepared, traumatised by WWI losses, sympathetic to revising Versailles, and feared communism more than Nazism.
items.misconception: Because Chamberlain was a coward and admired Hitler.
items.random_wrong: Because Britain had no army at all in the 1930s.
items.why: Hindsight makes appeasement look like weakness. In context it was a rational (if mistaken) strategy reflecting public opinion, financial constraints and genuine fear of another total war.

items.q: How did prison purposes change in the 19th century?
items.correct: Prisons shifted from holding before trial or transportation to punishment and reform via the Separate and Silent systems, influenced by Fry and the 1823 and 1865 Prison Acts.
items.misconception: Victorian prisons were created to rehabilitate prisoners kindly.
items.random_wrong: Victorian prisons abolished hard labour and corporal punishment.
items.why: Reform meant solitary confinement and hard labour intended to break the will, not modern rehabilitation. Students project 21st-century welfare values onto Victorian discipline.

items.q: Why did the Schlieffen Plan fail in 1914?
items.correct: Belgian resistance delayed the German advance; Russia mobilised faster than expected; Moltke weakened the right wing; and the French rallied at the Marne.
items.misconception: The Schlieffen Plan failed because Britain entered the war.
items.random_wrong: The Schlieffen Plan failed because it tried to invade Russia first.
items.why: Britain's army was small in 1914; failure was about timing and logistics, not British intervention. Students overestimate the BEF's role in the initial defeat.

items.q: Which Bolshevik slogan summarised Lenin's April Theses?
items.why: Correct answer: Peace, Land, Bread. “Workers of the world unite” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What was the immediate cause of the February Revolution?
items.why: Correct answer: Bread shortages in Petrograd. “German invasion” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did the Russian Civil War end?
items.why: The real figure is 1921 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
